1.3 Common Ion Effect>1.3.1 Common Ion Effect
1.4 Hydrolysis of Salts>1.4.2 Weak Acid + Strong Base

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The autoionization of water is represented by the equation \(2\mathrm{H_2O(l)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H_3O^+(aq)} + \mathrm{OH^-(aq)}\). At \(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), what is the value of the equilibrium constant \(K_w = [\mathrm{H_3O^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}]\)?
questions.hint: Recall the ion product constant for water at 25°C.
questions.explanation: At \(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\), \(K_w = [\mathrm{H_3O^+}][\mathrm{OH^-}] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14}\). Option A is \([\mathrm{H_3O^+}]\) in pure water, not \(K_w\).

explanations_tiered.definition: Autoionization is the reaction of two identical molecules to produce ions; for water: \(2\mathrm{H_2O} \rightarrow \mathrm{H_3O^+} + \mathrm{OH^-}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Kw, the ion product constant, equals \(1.0 \times 10^{-14}\) at 25°C and varies with temperature.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of water molecules occasionally breaking apart into H₃O⁺ and OH⁻, but only a tiny amount.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equilibrium constant expression excludes liquid water because its activity is 1. \(K_w\) is temperature-dependent; at 37°C, \(K_w = 2.5 \times 10^{-14}\).
misconceptions: Some confuse Kw with [H⁺] in pure water (10⁻⁷ M).

glossary_entry.definition: Ion product constant for water, 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following species can act as either a Brønsted-Lowry acid or a Brønsted-Lowry base depending on the reaction conditions?
questions.hint: Look for a species that can both donate and accept a proton.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{HCO_3^-}\) is amphiprotic: it can donate H⁺ to become \(\mathrm{CO_3^{2-}}\) or accept H⁺ to become \(\mathrm{H_2CO_3}\). Options A, C, D are neither proton donors nor acceptors under normal conditions.

source_trace.content_anchor: HCO₃⁻ acts as an acid in presence of OH⁻ but as a base in presence of HF

explanations_tiered.definition: An amphiprotic species can either donate or accept a proton, depending on the other reactant.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Water is the most common amphiprotic species; \(\mathrm{HCO_3^-}\), \(\mathrm{HSO_4^-}\), and \(\mathrm{H_2PO_4^-}\) are also amphiprotic.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An amphiprotic species is like a double agent — it can give away an H⁺ or take one.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\mathrm{HCO_3^-}\) can lose H⁺ to form \(\mathrm{CO_3^{2-}}\) (acting as acid) or gain H⁺ to form \(\mathrm{H_2CO_3}\) (acting as base).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Amphiprotic species have at least one ionizable hydrogen and a lone pair or negative charge to accept H⁺. The conjugate pairs are \(\mathrm{HCO_3^-/CO_3^{2-}}\) and \(\mathrm{H_2CO_3/HCO_3^-}\).
reasoning_steps: Identify species with both H to donate and ability to accept H⁺.
HCO₃⁻ fits: H to lose, lone pairs/negative charge to gain H⁺.
misconceptions: Students think all polyatomic ions with H are amphiprotic; e.g., HSO₄⁻ is, but H₂PO₄⁻ is too — check if conjugate pair exists.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Amphiprotic = can donate OR accept H⁺.

glossary_entry.definition: A species that can act as either a proton donor or proton acceptor.
glossary_entry.example_equation: \(\mathrm{HCO_3^- + OH^- \rightarrow CO_3^{2-} + H_2O}\) and \(\mathrm{HCO_3^- + HF \rightarrow H_2CO_3 + F^-}\)

flashcard.back: Can donate or accept a proton (H⁺).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Can donate or accept H⁺ (e.g., HCO₃⁻).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Species that can act as acid or base.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with amphoteric (acidic or basic oxides).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. HCO₃⁻ can donate or accept H⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In the reaction \(\mathrm{BF_3 + NH_3 \rightarrow F_3B:NH_3}\), which species acts as the Lewis acid and why?

options.A: \(\mathrm{NH_3}\), because it donates an electron pair
options.B: \(\mathrm{BF_3}\), because it accepts an electron pair

questions.hint: Lewis acid is an electron-pair acceptor.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{BF_3}\) has an incomplete octet (6 electrons) and accepts a lone pair from \(\mathrm{NH_3}\), making it the Lewis acid. Option A describes a Lewis base.

source_trace.content_anchor: BF₃ has only six electrons and accepts an electron pair from NH₃

explanations_tiered.definition: A Lewis acid is an electron-pair acceptor; a Lewis base is an electron-pair donor.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Lewis theory extends acid-base chemistry beyond protons: any species with an empty orbital (acid) and any with a lone pair (base) can react.
explanations_tiered.beginner: BF₃ is like an empty shelf (needs electrons); NH₃ is like a box of electrons to put on the shelf.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Boron in BF₃ has only 6 valence electrons, so it accepts the lone pair from NH₃. BF₃ is the Lewis acid; NH₃ is the Lewis base.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Lewis acidity is often measured by the acceptor number or by complex formation constants. BF₃ is a strong Lewis acid due to boron's electron deficiency.
reasoning_steps: Identify species with incomplete octet: BF₃.
misconceptions: Some think all Lewis acids contain H⁺; BF₃ has no H but is a strong Lewis acid.

glossary_entry.definition: A species that accepts an electron pair to form a covalent bond.

flashcard.front: What is the Lewis acid in BF₃ + NH₃ → F₃B:NH₃?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why is BF₃ a Lewis acid?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Accepts electron pair; boron has incomplete octet.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. BF₃ accepts electron pair from NH₃.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A solution has \([\mathrm{OH^-}] = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{M}\) at \(25^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\). What is the pH of this solution?
questions.hint: First find pOH = -log[OH⁻], then use pH + pOH = 14.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{pOH} = -\log(1.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 4.00\). \(\mathrm{pH} = 14.00 - \mathrm{pOH} = 14.00 - 4.00 = 10.00\). Option A is pOH, not pH.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The relationship comes from taking -log of Kw: -log[H₃O⁺] - log[OH⁻] = -log(1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴) = 14.
explanations_tiered.beginner: pOH tells how basic; pH tells how acidic. Add them to always get 14.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This relationship holds only at 25°C because Kw changes with temperature, altering the sum.
misconceptions: Some use pH = 14 - pOH but forget it's only at 25°C.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: A \(0.200 \ \mathrm{M}\) solution of a weak acid has \([\mathrm{H_3O^+}] = 4.0 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{M}\). What is the percent ionization of the acid?
questions.explanation: Percent ionization = \((4.0 \times 10^{-3}) / 0.200 \times 100\% = 0.02 \times 100\% = 2.0\%\). Option B would result from dividing by 0.400 incorrectly.

source_trace.content_anchor: Percent ionization = (ionized acid concentration / initial concentration) × 100%

explanations_tiered.definition: Percent ionization is the percentage of weak acid molecules that dissociate into ions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Percent ionization increases as acid becomes more dilute and as Ka increases.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Out of 100 acid molecules, percent ionization tells how many actually break apart.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\frac{4.0 \times 10^{-3}}{0.200} \times 100\% = 2.0\%\). Only 2% of the acid molecules ionized.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Percent ionization = \(\frac{[H^+]_{eq}}{C_0} \times 100\%\). For weak acids, it's usually <5% and increases with dilution.
misconceptions: Some use equilibrium [HA] instead of initial, giving a different value.

glossary_entry.definition: Fraction of weak acid molecules that ionize, expressed as a percentage.

flashcard.front: Formula for percent ionization of weak acid?

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize weak acids have low % ionization.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 1.4.3 Hydrolysis of Salts of Strong Acids and Weak Bases
questions.structural_anchor: Acidic salt from strong acid + weak base
questions.question: A solution of ammonium chloride \(\mathrm{NH_4Cl}\) in water is acidic because the ammonium ion reacts with water. Which equation correctly represents this hydrolysis reaction?
questions.hint: The ammonium ion donates a proton to water, forming hydronium ion.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{NH_4^+}\) is the conjugate acid of weak base \(\mathrm{NH_3}\) and hydrolyzes: \(\mathrm{NH_4^+ + H_2O \rightarrow NH_3 + H_3O^+}\), increasing [H₃O⁺]. Option A shows neutralization, not hydrolysis.

source_trace.structural_element: Acidic salt from strong acid + weak base

explanations_tiered.definition: Hydrolysis is the reaction of an ion with water to produce H₃O⁺ or OH⁻.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Salts of strong acids and weak bases yield acidic solutions because the cation (conjugate acid of weak base) releases H⁺ to water.
explanations_tiered.beginner: NH₄⁺ is like a tiny acid that gives an H⁺ to water, making H₃O⁺ and leaving NH₃ behind.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: NH₄⁺ donates a proton to H₂O: \(\mathrm{NH_4^+ + H_2O \rightarrow NH_3 + H_3O^+}\). H₃O⁺ makes solution acidic.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equilibrium constant for this hydrolysis is \(K_a(\mathrm{NH_4^+}) = K_w / K_b(\mathrm{NH_3}) = 10^{-14} / (1.8 \times 10^{-5}) = 5.6 \times 10^{-10}\), confirming weak acidity.
reasoning_steps: Identify cation from weak base: NH₄⁺.
Write reaction with water producing H₃O⁺.
misconceptions: Some write NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, but OH⁻ is not initially present; water is the reactant.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: NH₄Cl acidic: NH₄⁺ + H₂O → NH₃ + H₃O⁺.

glossary_entry.definition: Salt that produces acidic solution due to hydrolysis of cation.
glossary_entry.example_equation: \(\mathrm{NH_4Cl \rightarrow NH_4^+ + Cl^-; NH_4^+ + H_2O \rightarrow NH_3 + H_3O^+}\)

publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. NH₄⁺ donates H⁺ to H₂O forming H₃O⁺.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 1.4.4 Hydrolysis of Salts of Weak Acids and Weak Bases
questions.structural_anchor: pH of salt from weak acid and weak base
questions.question: For a salt formed from a weak acid and a weak base, the solution will be neutral if which condition is true?

options.A: \(K_a\) of the weak acid equals \(K_b\) of the weak base
options.D: The salt is always neutral regardless of \(K_a\) and \(K_b\)

questions.hint: The pH depends on the relative strengths of the conjugate acid and conjugate base formed.
questions.explanation: If \(K_a\) of the weak acid equals \(K_b\) of the weak base, the cation and anion hydrolyze equally, producing \([\mathrm{H_3O^+}] = [\mathrm{OH^-}]\), so the solution is neutral. Option B gives acidic; option C gives basic.

source_trace.structural_element: pH of salt from weak acid and weak base
source_trace.content_anchor: if Ka > Kb, acidic; if Ka < Kb, basic; if equal, neutral

explanations_tiered.definition: For a salt of weak acid and weak base, the cation hydrolyzes (as an acid) and the anion hydrolyzes (as a base); the pH depends on Ka vs Kb.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If Ka(acid) = Kb(base), then [H⁺] from cation equals [OH⁻] from anion, pH = 7.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Both parts of the salt fight with water — if they fight equally, the solution stays neutral.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The cation (conjugate acid of weak base) has Ka = Kw/Kb(weak base). The anion (conjugate base of weak acid) has Kb = Kw/Ka(weak acid). If Ka(acid) = Kb(base), then the two effects cancel, pH = 7.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The exact [H⁺] ≈ √(Kw × Ka(acid) / Kb(base)). For neutrality, Ka(acid) = Kb(base). Example: ammonium acetate (NH₄CH₃COO) is nearly neutral because Ka(NH₄⁺) ≈ Kb(CH₃COO⁻).
reasoning_steps: Compare Ka of cation acid with Kb of anion base.
misconceptions: Students often assume all salts of weak + weak are neutral, which is false.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Weak + weak salt: Ka = Kb → neutral.

glossary_entry.definition: Salt whose cation and anion both hydrolyze; pH depends on Ka vs Kb.

flashcard.front: When is a weak+weak salt neutral?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When is a weak+weak salt neutral?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Salt with hydrolyzing cation and anion.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Equal Ka and Kb give neutral.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: An indicator HIn has a pKa of 5.0. Its acid form (HIn) is yellow and its conjugate base (In⁻) is blue. What color would the indicator appear in a solution with pH = 4.0?
questions.hint: When pH < pKa, the acid form (HIn) dominates.
questions.explanation: At pH = 4.0, which is less than pKa = 5.0, the equilibrium \(\mathrm{HIn \rightleftharpoons H^+ + In^-}\) shifts left. HIn (yellow) predominates, so the solution appears yellow. Option B would occur at pH > pKa.
formula_used: Henderson-Hasselbalch for indicator: pH = pKa + log([In^-]/[HIn])

explanations_tiered.definition: Acid-base indicators are weak acids whose conjugate base has a different color.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When pH < pKa, [HIn] > [In⁻]; when pH > pKa, [In⁻] > [HIn]; color changes over ~2 pH units centered at pKa.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the solution is more acidic than the indicator's pKa, the indicator looks like its acid color (yellow).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: pH = pKa + log([In⁻]/[HIn]) → at pH 4.0, pKa 5.0: -1 = log([In⁻]/[HIn]) → [In⁻]/[HIn] = 0.1, so HIn dominates → yellow.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The ratio [In⁻]/[HIn] = 10^(pH-pKa) = 10^(-1) = 0.1, so 91% HIn, 9% In⁻. Yellow dominates.
reasoning_steps: If pH < pKa, acid form dominates.
misconceptions: Some think color changes exactly at pKa; actually it's gradual over ~2 pH units.

glossary_entry.definition: pH at which [HIn] = [In⁻]; midpoint of color change range.

flashcard.front: pH=4, pKa=5, acid color yellow, base color blue. What color?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: pH=4, indicator pKa=5, acid color yellow, what color?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: pH range over which indicator changes color.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. pH 4.0 < pKa 5.0 → HIn (yellow) dominates.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: In an acid-base titration, what is the difference between the equivalence point and the end point?

options.B: Equivalence point is theoretical; end point is observed via indicator
options.C: End point is theoretical; equivalence point is observed
options.D: Equivalence point occurs before the end point

questions.hint: One is calculated, one is measured.
questions.explanation: The equivalence point is the theoretical point where moles of acid equal moles of base. The end point is the actual point where the indicator changes color; ideally they are very close. Option D is incorrect because a good titration has them nearly simultaneous.

source_trace.content_anchor: equivalence point is where moles of base = moles of acid; end point is where indicator changes color

explanations_tiered.definition: Equivalence point = stoichiometric equality of H⁺ and OH⁻; end point = observed color change of indicator.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A suitable indicator has an end point very close to the equivalence point to minimize titration error.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Equivalence is the perfect math moment; end point is when you see the color change.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: At equivalence, moles H⁺ = moles OH⁻. End point is when indicator changes, ideally within ±0.02 mL of equivalence.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The difference (end point - equivalence) is titration error. For strong acid-strong base, any indicator with pKa near 7 works; for weak acids, choose indicator whose pH range includes equivalence pH.
reasoning_steps: Good titration makes them nearly equal.
misconceptions: Students often think end point = equivalence point; they are close but not identical in real titrations.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Equivalence point = ideal; end point = measured.

glossary_entry.definition: Point where moles of acid = moles of base.

flashcard.front: Difference between equivalence point and end point?
flashcard.back: Equivalence is theoretical; end point is observed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Equivalence=theoretical stoichiometry; end point=observed color change.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: End point ≈ equivalence point with good indicator.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish equivalence point and end point
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Equivalence point is calculated; end point is observed with indicator.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: If \(25.0 \ \mathrm{mL}\) of a \(0.10 \ \mathrm{M}\) \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) solution is neutralized by \(50.0 \ \mathrm{mL}\) of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) solution, what is the normality of the \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) solution?
questions.hint: For H₂SO₄, molarity × 2 = normality. Use N₁V₁ = N₂V₂.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{H_2SO_4}\) is diprotic: \(N_{\mathrm{acid}} = 0.10 \ \mathrm{M} \times 2 = 0.20 \ \mathrm{N}\). Then \(N_{\mathrm{base}} = N_{\mathrm{acid}}V_{\mathrm{acid}} / V_{\mathrm{base}} = (0.20 \ \mathrm{N} \times 25.0 \ \mathrm{mL}) / 50.0 \ \mathrm{mL} = 0.10 \ \mathrm{N}\). Option B is the acid normality, not base.
formula_used: N = M × n (n = H⁺ per formula)

explanations_tiered.definition: Normality = equivalents of solute per liter; for acids, equivalents = moles × H⁺ per molecule.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: H₂SO₄ has 2 H⁺, so 0.10 M = 0.20 N. Using N₁V₁ = N₂V₂ gives NaOH normality.
explanations_tiered.beginner: H₂SO₄ is twice as strong per mole, so its normality is double its molarity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Normality is reaction-dependent; here neutralization of H₂SO₄ to SO₄²⁻ uses both protons, so n-factor = 2.
worked_solution: Step 1: Find N_H2SO4 = 0.10 mol/L × 2 eq/mol = 0.20 eq/L = 0.20 N.
Step 2: N₁V₁ = N₂V₂ → N_NaOH = (0.20 N × 25.0 mL) / 50.0 mL = 0.10 N.

variables.meaning: Number of H⁺ or OH⁻ per formula

misconceptions: Many forget to convert molarity to normality for H₂SO₄.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find normality of NaOH that neutralizes 25 mL 0.10 M H₂SO₄ with 50 mL NaOH.
